content.problemStatement:
Chef decided to write an infinite sequence. Initially, he wrote 000, and then he started repeating the following process:


Look at the last element written so far (the lll-th element if the sequence has length lll so far); let's denote it by xxx.
If xxx does not occur anywhere earlier in the sequence, the next element in the sequence is 000.
Otherwise, look at the previous occurrence of xxx in the sequence, i.e. the kkk-th element, where k<lk \lt lk<l, this element is equal to xxx and all elements between the k+1k+1k+1-th and l−1l-1l−1-th are different from xxx. The next element is l−kl-kl−k, i.e. the distance between the last two occurrences of xxx.
The resulting sequence is (0,0,1,0,2,0,2,2,1,…)(0, 0, 1, 0, 2, 0, 2, 2, 1, \ldots)(0,0,1,0,2,0,2,2,1,…): the second element is 000 since 000 occurs only once in the sequence (0)(0)(0), the third element is 111 since the distance between the two occurrences of 000 in the sequence (0,0)(0, 0)(0,0) is 111, the fourth element is 000 since 111 occurs only once in the sequence (0,0,1)(0, 0, 1)(0,0,1), and so on.

Chef has given you a task to perform. Consider the NNN-th element of the sequence (denoted by xxx) and the first NNN elements of the sequence. Find the number of occurrences of xxx among these NNN elements.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains a single integer NNN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ― the number of occurrences of the NNN-th element.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The 222-nd element is 000. It occurs twice among the first two elements, since the first two elements are both 000.
